input_dict.agent_role: Summarize first-hop evidence and expose the bridge entity, missing fact, or retrieval direction needed for the next hop.

task_context.question: Which cartridge did John Browning design that has a rim that is the same in diameter as the .50 GI?
task_context.agent_role: Summarize first-hop evidence and expose the bridge entity, missing fact, or retrieval direction needed for the next hop.

visible_agent_inputs.question: Which cartridge did John Browning design that has a rim that is the same in diameter as the .50 GI?
passages: .50 GI | The .50 GI (12.7×23mmRB) pistol cartridge was developed by Vic Tibbets and Alex Zimmermann of Guncrafter Industries.  The .50 GI was introduced at the 2004 SHOT Show alongside the Guncrafter Industries Model No. 1, a variation of the M1911.  The round has a rebated rim that is the same diameter as that of the .45 ACP.

Guncrafter Industries Model No. 1 | The Guncrafter Industries Model No. 1 (Or M1) is a variant of the widely popular M1911 handgun, modified for the .50 GI cartridge.  John Browning's 1911 .45 ACP has been used for many cartridges over the past century.  In every instance the cartridge used a bullet with a diameter that was the same or smaller than the original .451".  Both the Desert Eagle and the LAR Grizzly have been offered in cartridges of .50 caliber.  Both are large cartridges requiring a large, heavy pistol.

M2 Browning | The M2 Machine Gun or Browning .50 Caliber Machine Gun is a heavy machine gun designed toward the end of World War I by John Browning.  Its design is similar to Browning's earlier M1919 Browning machine gun, which was chambered for the .30-06 cartridge.  The M2 uses the much larger and much more powerful .50 BMG cartridge, which was developed alongside and takes its name from the gun itself (BMG standing for "Browning Machine Gun").  It has been referred to as "Ma Deuce", in reference to its M2 nomenclature.  The design has had many specific designations; the official designation for the current infantry type is Browning Machine Gun, Cal.  .50, M2, HB, Flexible.  It is effective against infantry, unarmored or lightly armored vehicles and boats, light fortifications and low-flying aircraft.  The M2 has been produced longer than any other machine gun.

.585 Gehringer | The .585 Gehringer is a wildcat elephant gun cartridge based on the .585 Nyati.  The cartridge retains the .585" bullet diameter but the case has been lengthened and the rim diameter reduced to fit bolt faces designed for cartridges similar to the .416 Rigby.  It was designed over several years by Karl Gehringer, member of the Australian wrestling team, during service in the Australian Armed Forces.

.380 ACP | The .380 ACP (9x17mm) (Automatic Colt Pistol) is a rimless, straight-walled pistol cartridge developed by firearms designer John Moses Browning.  The cartridge headspaces on the mouth of the case.  It was introduced in 1908 by Colt, for use in its new Colt Model 1908 pocket hammerless semi-automatic, and has been a popular self-defense cartridge ever since, seeing wide use in numerous handguns (typically smaller weapons).  Other names for .380 ACP include .380 Auto, 9mm Browning, 9mm Corto, 9mm Kurz, 9mm Short, 9×17mm and 9 mm Browning Court (which is the C.I.P. designation).  It is not to be confused with .38 ACP, 9mm Ultra, 9×18mm Makarov, 9×19mm Parabellum nor the rimmed .380 Long Revolver CF & .380 Rook Rifle cartridges, introduced around 1870.

.32 ACP | .32 ACP (Automatic Colt Pistol), also known as the .32 Automatic is a centerfire pistol cartridge.  It is a semi-rimmed, straight-walled cartridge developed by firearms designer John Browning, initially for use in the FN M1900 semi-automatic pistol.  It was introduced in 1899 by Fabrique Nationale, and is also known as the 7.65×17mmSR Browning or 7.65 mm Browning Short.

Automatic Colt Pistol | Automatic Colt Pistol (ACP) denotes various John Browning cartridge designs primarily used in Colt and Fabrique Nationale de Herstal semi-automatic pistols.  All of these cartridges are straight-sided and appear similar.  The .25 ACP, .32 ACP, and .38 ACP are semi-rimmed and headspace on the rim, while the rimless .380 ACP and .45 ACP headspace on the mouth of the case.
visible_agent_inputs.current_output:
Evidence found:
- ".50 GI" — [1]: "The round has a rebated rim that is the same diameter as that of the .45 ACP."
- ".45 ACP" — [1]: "the .45 ACP."
- "John Browning" — [2]: "John Browning's 1911 .45 ACP"
- ".45 ACP" attribution — [7]: "the rimless .380 ACP and .45 ACP headspace on the mouth."

Ambiguities:
- None; the passages consistently link the .50 GI rim to the .45 ACP and attribute the .45 ACP to John Browning.

Bridge / missing fact:
- No missing bridge facts. [1] gives rim-equality to ".45 ACP" and [2]/[7] attribute ".45 ACP" to John Browning. Final answer: .45 ACP.

Retrieval direction:
- Page: "45 ACP"
- Page: "John Browning"
- Page: ".50 GI"
- (No deferral — passages supply the needed facts.)

input_dict.current_prompt:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.

request.rendered_prompt:
You are deriving one LOCAL PROMPT DELTA Δp_i for an agent in a fixed
multi-agent HotpotQA pipeline.

The source and target states describe one local functional transition.
Produce a prompt-update rule that helps the unchanged base prompt move
through this edge. The delta will later be placed in an ORDERED list with
all other edge deltas and executed directly as supplemental context.

Return JSON only:
{
  "delta_p": {
    "trigger": "...",
    "preserve_rule": "...",
    "change_rule": "...",
    "drop_or_avoid_rule": "...",
    "output_contract": "...",
    "why_local_transition": "..."
  },
  "rationale": "..."
}

Strict anti-cheating rules:
- Δp_i is a PROMPT EDIT / behavior rule, never the target output.
- Do not output a candidate answer, summary, or query as Δp_i.
- Do not instruct the agent to copy the target state.
- Do not copy the gold answer, hidden support title, target-only entity,
  or a long literal span that appears only in the target state.
- Sample-specific anchors may appear only when they already occur in the
  visible agent inputs below.
- Do not mention gold labels, oracle labels, evaluation metrics, missing
  support, target state, source state, or endpoint reconstruction.
- Preserve the agent's original input/output interface.
- Express the smallest coherent behavioral change for this edge.
- Preserve useful behavior and evidence already present at the source.
- Each edge is inverted independently from the same current prompt.
